ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for mutton kofta curry; mapped to nearest meatball food product concept as closest parent. Curry sauce component and mutton species specificity are not captured in this code.
ontology.foodex2_description: Mutton, cooked — used as closest FoodEx2 anchor for mutton-based prepared dish; curry sauce and meatball form are not individually coded.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for mutton kofta curry as a composite dish; A0CKP (mutton, cooked) is the closest defensible parent anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept identified for mutton kofta curry as a composite prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of mutton kofta curry is approximately 200g, comprising 4–5 meatballs with curry gravy, as commonly served in a Singaporean mamak or Indian-Muslim restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Mutton Kofta Curry — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Mutton Kofta Curry (cr_id F06060014). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Meat and Curry Dishes — analogous estimate based on low-carbohydrate meat curry reference
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for mutton kofta curry. GI estimated at approximately 35 (low) based on analogous low-carbohydrate meat curry dishes in the University of Sydney GI Database. The dish has only 5.2g carbohydrate per 100g, predominantly from onion, tomato, and spices, which are low-GI ingredients. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (35 × 10.4) / 100 = 3.6.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 685mg per 100g — exceeds the 600mg/100g upper threshold for hypertension-sensitive individuals
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid mutton kofta curry in standard preparation; request a reduced-salt version and limit portion to no more than 100g, pairing with unsalted vegetables to partially offset sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (5.2g/100g) is favourable, but high total fat (15.6g/100g) including saturated fat (~6.5g/100g) may impair insulin sensitivity with regular consumption
type2_diabetes.guidance: Mutton kofta curry is acceptable for people with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions due to its low carbohydrate content, but should be requested with reduced oil to limit saturated fat, and should not be paired with large servings of white rice or refined carbohydrates.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~6.5g/100g from mutton fat and cooking oil) and high sodium (685mg/100g) both elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: People with cardiovascular disease should consume mutton kofta curry only occasionally and in small portions; request lean mutton preparation with minimal oil, and avoid pairing with other high-sodium or high-fat foods in the same meal.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Mutton is red meat rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6) which amplifies inflammatory cytokines; saturated fat exceeds 5g/100g threshold and sodium at 685mg/100g worsens tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit or avoid mutton kofta curry due to its pro-inflammatory red meat content and high saturated fat; turmeric and ginger in the curry provide some anti-inflammatory benefit but do not offset the overall inflammatory burden of the dish.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mutton Kofta Curry is an Indian-style dish consisting of spiced minced mutton meatballs (kofta) simmered in a rich, aromatic curry gravy. The kofta are made from a mixture of lean and fatty mutton mince combined with onion, garlic, ginger, and spices such as cumin, coriander, turmeric, and chilli, then shaped into balls and shallow-fried before being cooked in a tomato-based curry sauce. The dish is a staple of Mamak (Indian-Muslim) cuisine in Singapore and is commonly served at mamak stalls and Indian-Muslim restaurants. The texture is tender and juicy with a deeply spiced, slightly oily gravy. Nutritionally, per 100g it provides 205 kcal, 10.7g protein, 15.6g fat (including approximately 6.5g saturated fat), 5.2g carbohydrate, 0.8g dietary fibre, and 685mg sodium. The high sodium content (685mg/100g) makes it unsuitable for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease without significant salt reduction. The high saturated fat and red meat content raise concerns for cardiovascular disease and rheumatoid arthritis. For people with Type 2 Diabetes, the low carbohydrate content is favourable, though the fat content warrants moderation. The dish is halal and gluten-free. It is typically served with white rice, roti, or naan in a Singaporean mamak context.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F06060014) at confidence 0.95. The food is an exact match in the primary regional source. Saturated fat and sugar are estimated from standard mutton curry composition references consistent with HPB data. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for low-carbohydrate meat curry dishes.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, full micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific mutton kofta curry preparation are not individually reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for F06060014 as of 2026; these values are also not available in USDA FoodData Central for an equivalent composite mutton kofta curry dish. Saturated_fat_g estimated at 6.5g/100g based on standard mutton fat composition (approximately 42% of total fat as saturated) consistent with USDA FoodData Central mutton data. Sugar_g estimated at 2.5g/100g from onion and tomato content in the curry gravy, consistent with HPB composite dish methodology. GI is an analogous estimate (GI ~35, low) from the University of Sydney GI Database for comparable low-carbohydrate meat curry dishes; no direct GI measurement for mutton kofta curry was found in approved sources. Ontology: FoodOn mapped to nearest meatball food product concept (closest_parent match, gap noted); FoodEx2 A0CKP (mutton, cooked) used as closest defensible parent anchor for this composite dish (closest_parent match, gap noted).
